title: Game 7: G. Kasparov vs A. Karpov
fullText:
Bring Up the Reserves

In the case of an attack on the king, the two main factors determining the success of an attack are the balance of forces in the vicinity of the king, and the state of the king’s defences. If the king is severely exposed and lacking defenders, even a relatively modest attacking force (such as a queen and a knight) may be enough to decide the game. However, it is more common for the king to have some defensive pieces and shielding pawns. In this case the attacker needs a considerable local superiority of force in order to break through. It follows that the more pieces take part in the attack, the more likely it is to succeed.

One of the most common attacking errors is to start an attack with insufficient force to drive the attack home. By the time this becomes obvious, it is too late to bring up the reserves. It is therefore a good idea to bring as many pieces as possible into aggressive positions before making an irrevocable commitment to the attack. It may not be possible to do this with every piece, but make whatever preparations you can. It is better to bring a piece into the attack before, rather than after, sacrificing a piece!

The following game is a good example of this principle in action.

G. Kasparov – A. Karpov

World Championship match (game 20), New York/Lyons 1990

Ruy Lopez, Flohr-Zaitsev Variation

1 e4

For comments on this move, see Game 3.

1...e5

This symmetrical response is the traditional answer to White’s 1 e4. Although to some extent it has been overshadowed by the immense popularity of the Sicilian Defence (1...c5 – see Game 8, amongst others), it remains a very common and reliable reply. By controlling d4, Black tries to stop White forming a two-abreast pawn-centre.

2 Nf3

Overwhelmingly the most popular move, attacking the e5-pawn. If White is obstinate and plays 2 d4 in any case, then after 2...exd4 3 Qxd4 Nc6 White has to lose time with his queen. That is not to say that this line is unplayable – indeed, such strong players as Adams and Morozevich have experimented with it – but the prevailing view is that having to move the queen twice in the first four moves gives Black a relatively easy time. Other second moves, such as 2 f4 (King’s Gambit), 2 Nc3 (Vienna Opening) and 2 Bc4 (Bishop’s Opening) are also relatively rare in contemporary tournament play.

2...Nc6

Again the most common move, although the counter-attacking 2...Nf6 (Petroff’s Defence) is running it a close second at the higher levels of grandmaster chess. With 2...Nf6, Black is aiming to neutralize White’s advantage by an early liquidation in the centre, and so this move tends to lead to less exciting positions than 2...Nc6, which maintains the tension in the centre for longer. Whether this is an advantage or a defect depends on your point of view. In top-class grandmaster chess, a draw with Black is generally regarded as a desirable objective, hence the popularity of 2...Nf6 at this level. In recent times White has certainly found it hard to prove any real advantage against it.

For players lower down the ranking list, whether to play 2...Nf6 or 2...Nc6 depends much more on personal style. Minute differences in theoretical opinion are much less important than reaching a position of a type that you play well. For top GMs, who can normally play all types of position well, such subjective factors are less important.

3 Bb5

This opening, the Ruy Lopez (or Spanish, as it is called in many countries) has been regarded as a sound system for the past 100 years, and this opinion is unlikely to change in the next 100 years. Such classic openings are perennially popular, largely because they are not really a single opening. The Ruy Lopez, for example, consists of a whole complex of different systems; if one falls out of favour, there are plenty of others to provide a viable replacement. The text-move prepares a possible exchange of the c6-knight, thereby removing the only defender of the e5-pawn. White is not immediately threatening to win a pawn (see the note to White’s fourth move), but this possibility is a constant cause of concern for Black.

The main alternative is 3 d4, the Scotch Opening (see Game 16), which is similar to 2 d4 except that White avoids the problems associated with an early queen development (after 3...exd4 4 Nxd4 Nxd4 5 Qxd4 it is not easy for Black to harass the queen). While Kasparov has had some success with the Scotch, other players do not seem able to emulate his results.

3...a6

This is the most popular reply. At this moment White cannot win a pawn by taking on c6, so Black takes the opportunity to kick the bishop back to a4. Then Black will have the option of removing the attack against c6 by playing ...b5. Another sound system is 3...Nf6 (the Berlin Defence) although, like 2...Nf6, this line often leads to rather dull positions.

4 Ba4

4 Bxc6 dxc6 5 0-0 (after 5 Nxe5, Black regains the pawn by 5...Qd4) genuinely threatens the e5-pawn, but Black has various possible counters: 5...f6, 5...Bg4, 5...Qd6 and 5...Bd6 are all reasonable replies. This Exchange Variation suits white-players who like quiet positions and endgame play.

4...Nf6

Black develops his knight to the most natural square, at the same time attacking White’s e4-pawn.

5 0-0

White is able to ignore the attack and continue his development, because if Black takes on e4, White can regain his pawn by force.

5...Be7

This move leads to the Closed Variation of the Ruy Lopez. The main alternative is 5...Nxe4, the Open Variation. Although the Open Variation is not especially popular, there is nothing really wrong with it; after 6 d4 b5 (6...exd4 is dubious since after 7 Re1 d5 8 Nxd4 both black knights are awkwardly pinned) 7 Bb3 the greedy 7...exd4? 8 Re1 d5 favours White since 9 Nc3! Be6 (9...dxc3?! 10 Bxd5 is even worse for Black, while 9...Be7? loses a piece to 10 Bxd5) 10 Nxe4 dxe4 11 Rxe4 Be7 12 Bxe6 fxe6 13 Nxd4 gives Black nothing to show for his extremely weak e-pawn. Therefore Black does best to give the pawn back by 7...d5 8 dxe5 Be6, when an interesting position arises in which Black’s main asset is his active knight on e4. Despite many decades of analysis, the best continuation for White is far from clear, although most grandmasters seem to think that White can retain an edge.

6 Re1

White defends the e4-pawn and does not allow Black a second chance to take on e4. This move is stronger than the obvious 6 Nc3, because White still aims to set up a two-abreast pawn-centre with c3 and d4, and so he should avoid blocking the c-pawn. This plan is time-consuming, but if White succeeds in establishing and maintaining his pawn-centre, then he will have promising long-term chances.

6...b5

White was really threatening to win a pawn by 7 Bxc6 dxc6 8 Nxe5, so this is the appropriate moment to drive the bishop away.

7 Bb3

7...d6

Round about here Black must decide how he is going to counter White’s plan. The text-move indicates that Black intends to respond by reinforcing his grip on e5, to make sure that it can resist the pressure White will exert on that square when he plays d4.

The main alternative is 7...0-0 8 c3 d5, the famous Marshall Attack, whereby Black offers a pawn in return for a lead in development and kingside pressure. These days the Marshall Attack is still played occasionally, but is not regarded as especially fearsome. Not only is it possible (albeit double-edged) to accept the pawn, but also White can avoid the Marshall entirely by playing 8 a4 instead of 8 c3.

8 c3

This not only prepares d4, but also gives the bishop a chance to slip back to c2 in case it is attacked by ...Na5.

8...0-0

Black proceeds with his development and brings his king into safety.

9 h3

White continues his preparation for d4. If he plays 9 d4 at once, Black replies 9...Bg4 and White must make some concession. Black’s threat is to double White’s pawns by ...Bxf3 (since the reply Qxf3 would cost White his d-pawn). There are then two main possibilities for White. The first is 10 d5, but this gives up one of the main benefits of a two-abreast pawn-centre – its flexibility. The second is 10 Be3 but the bishop is not ideally placed here since it blocks the e-file. After 10...exd4 11 cxd4 Na5 12 Bc2 c5 Black’s lead in development compensates for White’s slightly superior central control.

9...Bb7

This is a key decision-point for Black. He cannot hold up White’s d4 advance any longer, so he has to start thinking about the post-d4 world. Although Black has to face the fact that White will be better placed in the centre, Black has a lead in development as compensation. He must put this to good use, because given time White will catch up in development, when Black will simply be worse because of the situation in the centre, which inherently favours White. There are two main plans Black can adopt:

1) To attack White’s centre using his pawns. Since ...d5 is unlikely to be playable owing to the weakness of e5, this means using ...c5 to put pressure on White’s d4-pawn, hoping to force a clarification of the central pawn-structure. There are two ways to implement this plan:

1a) 9...Na5 10 Bc2 c5 11 d4 Qc7 is the first. Black has already played ...c5, and he will quickly develop his remaining pieces by ...Bd7 and ...Rac8 (possibly with ...cxd4 thrown in). The pressure against d4 and along the c-file is quite awkward for White. As usual in the Closed Ruy Lopez, the question is whether White can withstand the temporary pressure and complete his development with his centre intact, or whether he has to play d5 or dxe5 under unfavourable circumstances.

1b) The other way is 9...Nb8 10 d4 Nbd7, the Breyer Defence. This line may look odd but it is actually quite logical. Black manoeuvres his knight to defend e5 from a square that does not block the thematic ...c5. After 11 Nbd2 Bb7 another benefit of Black’s knight manoeuvre is revealed: on d7, the knight does not block the long diagonal and therefore White’s e-pawn comes under unwelcome pressure. In order for White to complete his queenside development, he must move his d2-knight to f1, but at the moment this is impossible. Therefore White usually plays 12 Bc2, followed by Nf1-g3, but of course this represents a loss of time and gives Black the opportunity to strike out with a timely ...c5.

2) Black’s second plan is to interfere with White’s development using piece pressure against White’s centre. This means mounting a rapid attack against e4, so as to prevent the important Nbd2-f1 manoeuvre (rather as in the Breyer Defence above). Thus the bishop should move to b7; the attack against e4 is currently masked by the c6-knight, but White must still bear it in mind.

10 d4

White has succeeded in building a good pawn-centre, but the cost is a lag in development.

10...Re8

Black intends ...Bf8 to step up the pressure against e4. At first sight the text-move looks wrong, because White can play 11 Ng5, forcing Black to return his rook to f8. However, White cannot make any use of the knight on g5, and has nothing better than to return it to f3. This means that a draw by repetition is in White’s hands, if he wants it. Many games have finished like this, but most players are unwilling to concede the advantage of the white pieces so easily!

11 Nbd2

The pawn on c3 means that d2 is the best square available for the knight, despite the obvious defect of blocking in the c1-bishop and thus the a1-rook. If White could complete the manoeuvre Nf1-g3, then he would be able to support the e4-pawn without blocking in the bishop, so Black should try to prevent this.

11...Bf8

Black is just in time to set up an attack on e4.

12 a4

12 Nf1 is bad because of 12...exd4 13 cxd4 Na5, when White has no good reply to the triple attack on e4 (not, however, 13...Nxe4? due to 14 Rxe4! Rxe4 15 Ng5 Re7 16 Qh5 with a very strong attack). 12 Ng5 is again ineffective, this time because of 12...Re7. 12 Bc2 appears logical, the aim being to free the d2-knight to move to f1 and g3. However, 13 Nf1 isn’t really a threat, because after 13...exd4 14 cxd4 Nb4 Black threatens both the e-pawn and 15...Nxc2, eliminating a useful bishop.

Therefore, White switches to a different plan; he intends to develop queenside play while leaving the knight on d2. The text-move is useful because White’s rook may have to stay at a1 for a considerable time while he is sorting out the development of the c1-bishop. Playing a4 introduces the possibility that the rook may enter play along the a-file. Moreover, White may be able to step up the pressure against the b5-pawn and force Black to clarify the queenside pawn-structure.

12...h6

Observing that White has few useful moves, Black decides to spend a tempo preventing Ng5. However, this move is by no means forced and both 12...g6 and 12...Qd7 are playable.

13 Bc2

White has indeed run out of useful moves and must now concede this voluntary retreat. The intention is 14 Bd3, forcing Black to make an awkward decision regarding the attack on the b5-pawn.

13...exd4

This move is clearly very double-edged, because Black surrenders his central strongpoint on e5. On the other hand, it opens various possibilities for active piece-play. The e8-rook is now directly attacking the e4-pawn, and Black will be able to step up his pressure on White’s centre with gain of tempo. 13...Rb8 and 13...Nb8 are playable alternatives, although both have a slightly passive appearance.

14 cxd4

A hidden benefit of this capture is that it opens the third rank, increasing White’s chances of activating the a1-rook via a3.

14...Nb4

Black clears the way for ...c5, to try to break up White’s centre. Unlike the lines 9...Na5 and 9...Nb8 discussed in the note to Black’s 9th move, in this case Black frees the c-pawn with gain of tempo and occupies an excellent outpost with his knight.

15 Bb1

White must keep his bishop, since it is currently one of his few active pieces.

15...c5

It is perfectly natural for Black to strike at White’s centre like this, but the move has both good and bad features. On the minus side, after White’s reply the b7-bishop will be shut out of play. On the plus side, Black gains space and stabilizes the central pawn-structure.

16 d5

The only challenging move, maintaining a 2-1 central pawn-majority. The central pawn-structure now resembles one arising from a Benoni (1 d4 Nf6 2 c4 c5 3 d5 e6 4 Nc3 exd5 5 cxd5 d6) rather than a Ruy Lopez. However, the arrangement of pieces is not typical for a Benoni so we cannot transplant typical ideas and plans from the Benoni into the current situation.

In the diagram position, White’s ambitions lie mainly on the kingside. Although currently White’s pieces are not menacing Black’s kingside, it only requires a few moves – Ra3, Nh2 and Rg3 – and the threats already look quite serious. Black, for his part, will aim to make use of his queenside majority, for example by ...Nd7, ...c4 and ...Nc5 (aiming for d3), possibly coupled with ...g6 and ...Bg7. The arrival of a knight on d3 is a key feature of this system, not only because it is an excellent outpost in itself, but also because it would block the activity of the b1-bishop and so reduce the force of White’s attack.

16...Nd7

A flexible move. The first idea is ...c4 and ...Nc5, as mentioned previously; the second idea is revealed with Black’s next move.

17 Ra3

White makes the first ominous move towards Black’s kingside.

17...f5!?

Black ups the stakes with this double-edged move. One of the main principles of defending against an attack on the king is not to create unnecessary weaknesses, yet here is a top-class player lunging forwards in contravention of precisely this rule. In view of the battering Black receives in this game, it might seem that justice was indeed dispensed, yet in fact this move is not at all bad. Certainly, it is very risky, yet it has many positive features. It is quite likely that the e4-pawn will disappear (either through exf5 or ...fxe4), but then the d5-pawn will be weak. If Black can take it, then his formerly inactive b7-bishop will suddenly become very powerful. If White plays exf5, then an exchange of rooks will tend to reduce the force of White’s attack.

It is true that if White’s attack were further advanced, Black could not get away with a move such as ...f5, but White’s threats are as yet quite far away and so Black still has time to break up White’s centre. Indeed, ...f5 can be seen as a pre-emptive strike to fight for activity before White’s attack becomes too strong.

The alternative is 17...c4, aiming to play ...Nc5. This is perfectly playable and possibly safer than the text-move, although it does have the defect of allowing the f3-knight to occupy d4.

18 Rae3

Even today, nobody knows whether this is the strongest move. In addition to the text-move, 18 g4, 18 exf5, 18 e5 and (most recently) 18 Nh2 have all been tried with varying degrees of success. Perhaps this last move is most logical as White does not yet commit his rook to a particular square; it might move to e3, f3 or g3 according to circumstances. In any case, White’s main aim is to bring his remaining pieces into the attack without allowing too many exchanges. When attacking, it is essential to have enough firepower available to finish the job. What constitutes ‘enough firepower’ varies a lot from position to position. If the enemy kingside is weakened and lacks defensive pieces, then even a small attacking force may be enough. However, in practice it often happens that the attack is being conducted against a relatively solid kingside, and in this case more pieces must be brought to bear to ensure success.

White’s main problem is to coordinate his forces for the attack. The c1-bishop in particular is quite inactive, and is likely to remain so because the knight must stay on d2 to be ready to recapture on e4. Kasparov therefore moves his a3-rook to a more active position, preparing to activate the bishop by b3 and Bb2.

18...Nf6

The situation in the centre remains fluid for a number of moves, the reason being that neither player wishes to resolve the e4-f5 tension. Black, it is true, can win the d5-pawn by playing ...fxe4, but this capture brings the d2-knight to an aggressive position with gain of tempo. It also gives both white bishops an obstructed view of Black’s kingside. In the end, Black may be forced to make this capture, but he intends to make as many constructive moves as he can first. Likewise, the move exf5 would be a concession by White. True, this avoids losing a pawn, but more importantly it loses time, since the knight stays on d2, and the pawn on f5 can actually obstruct White’s attack. Thus White also would prefer to find constructive moves that do not involve resolving the central tension.

The alternatives to the text-move are:

1) 18...f4!? attempts to block the position. After 19 R3e2 Ne5 20 Nf1 Nxf3+ 21 gxf3 Qh4 a very unclear position arises. If Black can cement his hold on e5 then he will stand well, but there is a danger that White will unleash all the energy in his position by playing e5 himself.

2) 18...fxe4?! is a concession since after 19 Rxe4 (19 Bxe4 is doubtful because of 19...Nf6, while 19 Nxe4 Nxd5 20 R3e2 N7f6 is unclear) 19...Rxe4 20 Nxe4 Nxd5 21 Qe2 White’s c1-bishop has entered the attack. The fact that Black has won a pawn is less important. White now threatens 22 Nxd6, and Black has defensive difficulties; for example, after 21...N7f6 22 Nxf6+ Qxf6 (22...Nxf6 23 Qe6+ Kh8 24 Nh4 is crushing) 23 Qe4 Black can hardly prevent White’s queen from landing on h7 (23...g6 is met by 24 Nh4!).

19 Nh2

White decides to clear the third rank after all, and introduces the possibility of a later Ng4 (after the f5-pawn has disappeared) to eliminate a kingside defender. If this is really White’s best move, then it was surely better to play Nh2 last move, as the rook might want to move from a3 to g3 in one go.

19 exf5?! is wrong; after 19...Rxe3 20 Rxe3 Nbxd5 Black gains time and the f5-pawn blocks White’s attack.

19...Kh8?!

Only here does Black commit a perceptible inaccuracy. Tidying-up moves such as Kg1-h1 and Kc1-b1 (or ...Kg8-h8 and ...Kc8-b8 by Black) are of course common enough, but one must be sure that the gain is worth the tempo spent on the move. This is more likely to be the case when the position is relatively quiet and the tempo is not especially significant. Here, with a violent storm about to break over Black’s kingside, it looks very risky to spend a tempo on this move, which actually has little clear benefit. If White is going to hack his way into the kingside sacrificially, it probably isn’t going to make much difference whether the king is on g8 or h8. After the text-move, Black must proceed with extreme accuracy in order not to be wiped out by White’s attack.

19...fxe4 is again wrong. After 20 Nxe4 Nbxd5 (20...Nfxd5 21 Rg3 is extremely dangerous for Black; for example, 21...Kh8 22 Bxh6 gxh6 23 Qg4 Ne7 24 Nf6 with a quick mate) 21 Nxf6+ Nxf6 22 Rxe8 Nxe8 23 Qd3 Nf6 24 Ng4 White has an extremely strong attack.

Black should have played 19...Qd7 connecting the rooks, supporting the b5-pawn and offering some lateral defence along the second rank. I do not want to go into a deep discussion of what is still opening theory; suffice it to say that White has not been able to prove any advantage after 19...Qd7. This move was played in a later Kasparov-Karpov game (Euwe Memorial, Amsterdam 1991) and on that occasion Black equalized and the game ended in a draw.

20 b3!

In order for White’s attack to stand a chance of success, he needs to have as many pieces as possible participating. Leaving the situation in the centre to look after itself, Kasparov simply deploys his dark-squared bishop on the long diagonal. This is an especially tempting prospect with the enemy king sitting at the end of the diagonal.

20...bxa4!

Karpov correctly decides to play for an outpost at d3, so that he can block the b1-h7 diagonal at a moment of his choosing. Although this is a good plan, the long dark-square diagonal remains open, so the black king remains in danger even if White has to surrender his light-squared bishop.

Playing for the win of a pawn by 20...fxe4 is critical, but gives White a dangerous attack after 21 Nxe4. Then:

1) 21...Nbxd5 22 Nxf6 Rxe3 (22...Nxf6 23 Rxe8 Nxe8 24 Qd3 Nf6 25 Ng4 and 22...Nxe3 23 Qd3 win for White) 23 Rxe3 Nxf6 (23...Qxf6 24 Qc2 and 23...gxf6 24 Rg3 give White a decisive attack) 24 Ng4 and the weakness of the b1-h7 diagonal gives White a very strong attack (in this line the move ...Kh8 proves especially unfortunate as the king will have to return to g8 to avoid mate).

2) 21...Bxd5 22 Nxf6 Rxe3 23 Rxe3 Qxf6 24 Ng4 (24 Bd2 is less effective in view of 24...Bg8!) 24...Qd4 25 Bd2 (threatening 26 Nxh6) 25...Kg8 26 Qc1 with many threats, including 27 Bxb4 followed by 28 Qc2.

3) 21...Nfxd5 and now:

3a) 22 Nxd6 is wrong because of 22...Nxe3 23 Nf7+ Kg8 24 Nxd8 Nxd1 25 Rxe8 Bc6.

3b) 22 Rf3 (intending Ng4 coupled with Bb2) 22...Nf6 23 Rxf6!? gxf6 24 Ng4 is perhaps not entirely clear after 24...Bxe4 (24...Re6 25 Bb2 Bg7 26 Nexf6 is very bad for Black; for example, 26...Qe7 27 Rxe6 Qxe6 28 Qd2 {threatening 29 Nh5} 28...Qxb3 29 Nxh6 with a winning attack for White) 25 Rxe4 Rxe4 26 Bxe4 d5! (26...Ra7? 27 Bb2 Bg7 28 Qd2 Qf8 29 Qf4 looks very dangerous for Black). One line is 27 Bg6 d4 28 Nxh6 Bxh6 29 Qh5 Qe7 and now 30 Bxh6 can be met by 30...Qe1+ 31 Kh2 Qe5+.

3c) 22 Rg3!? Nf6 23 Nxf6 Rxe1+ (after 23...Qxf6 24 Ng4 Qd4 25 Bd2 Rxe1+ 26 Qxe1 Nd5 27 Nxh6!, 27...gxh6 28 Qe6 wins for White, while 27...Nf6 28 Bc3 Qf4 29 Nf7+ Kg8 30 Bg6 gives White a very strong attack for no material sacrifice) 24 Qxe1 Qxf6 25 Bd2 followed by Ng4 looks dangerous for Black.

Thus, winning the d5-pawn immediately would not have been better than the text-move.

21 bxa4

21...c4

Now Black is ready to play ...Nd3 when necessary. However, as this move inevitably costs a pawn, Black won’t play it until he has already taken the d5-pawn or White has sacrificed something on the kingside.

22 Bb2

22...fxe4

Having done all he can to improve his position, Black now goes for the d5-pawn. There is no better move. For example:

1) 22...Nd3 23 Bxd3 cxd3 24 Bxf6 (24 Qb3 Nxe4 25 Qxb7 Rb8 is unclear) 24...Qxf6 25 Qb3 and White wins a pawn.

2) 22...Rc8 23 Qf3! (after 23 Bc3 a5 White has dangerous attacking chances, but Black would still be in the game) 23...fxe4 (23...Qd7 24 Bxf6 gxf6 25 Rc1 favours White on account of Black’s many weak pawns) 24 Nxe4 and now:

2a) 24...Bxd5 25 Nxf6 Bxf3 26 Rxe8 Qxe8 (26...Qc7 loses to 27 Bf5! Rxe8 28 Rxe8 Qf7 29 Bg6) 27 Rxe8 Rxe8 28 Nxe8 Bc6 29 Nc7 Bxa4 30 Ng4 and the extra piece should be enough to win.

2b) 24...Nfxd5 25 Nxd6! and now:

2b1) 25...Nxe3 26 Nf7+ Kg8 27 Nxh6+ Kh8 (after 27...gxh6, 28 Bh7+! Kxh7 29 Qf7+ mates) 28 Nf7+ Kg8 29 Bh7+ Kxh7 30 Qh5+ Kg8 31 Nxd8 Rcxd8 32 fxe3 and White should win.

2b2) 25...Rxe3 26 Nf7+ Kg8 27 Nxh6+ Kh8 (27...gxh6 28 Bh7+) 28 fxe3 and White is a pawn up with a dangerous attack.

23 Nxe4

23...Nfxd5

The only possible way to take the d5-pawn. The alternatives fail as follows:

1) 23...Bxd5? 24 Nxf6 Rxe3 25 Nxd5 Rxe1+ 26 Qxe1 Nxd5 27 Qe4 with a winning position for White.

2) 23...Nbxd5?! 24 Nxf6 Rxe3 (24...Nxe3 25 Qh5 leaves Black defenceless against the threat of 26 Qg6; for example, 25...gxf6 26 Qg6 Be4 27 Bxe4 Rxe4 28 Bxf6+ Qxf6 29 Qxf6+ Bg7 30 Qg6 wins) 25 Rxe3 gxf6 (25...Nxe3 loses to 26 Qh5 as before; 25...Nxf6 26 Qc2 c3 27 Ng4 and White wins) 26 Qg4 Bg7 27 Rg3 Qg8 (27...Qe7 28 Qf5 gives White a winning attack) 28 Qxc4 and White has a large positional advantage in addition to a very strong attack.

24 Rg3

The correct method to continue to attack is to move White’s pieces quietly into attacking positions, rather than to go for an immediate knockout. For example, 24 Nxd6 fails to 24...Nxe3 25 Nf7+ Kg8 26 Nxd8 Nxd1 27 Rxe8 Nxb2, when Black wins, while after 24 Qh5 Black can continue 24...c3! 25 Bxc3 Nxc3 26 Nxc3 Rxe3 27 Rxe3 Qg5, escaping to an ending in which he is only slightly worse.

24...Re6!

This is the best defence. Black supports the third rank in anticipation of the coming Ng4, which will attack the h6-pawn. In some lines the black queen can move behind the rook, setting up an awkward pin along the e-file. Instead, 24...Nd3 forces a liquidation, but does not solve Black’s problems, as after 25 Bxd3 cxd3 26 Qh5 Re6 27 Rg6 Rxg6 28 Qxg6 Qe8 29 Qxh6+ Kg8 30 Qg5 d2 31 Qxd2 Qxa4 32 Qg5 Qb4 33 Re2 White retains a strong attack for no material compensation.

25 Ng4

25 Nf3 is tempting, heading for d4, but after 25...Nf4 26 Nd4 Re5 the position is not clear.

After the text-move, every single one of White’s pieces occupies an aggressive position. With so many attacking pieces, Black will be hard pressed to prevent some sort of sacrificial breakthrough on the kingside.

25...Qe8?

Up to here, Black has defended very well in a dangerous situation, but the strain of finding the correct move time after time finally tells on him. Karpov tries to defend by pinning the e4-knight against the rook, but it turns out that White can break through despite the pin. The alternatives are:

1) 25...Qh4? loses to 26 Nc5! Rxe1+ 27 Qxe1 dxc5 28 Qe4 Nd3 29 Rxd3.

2) 25...Qd7? 26 Qc1! (26 Nef6? Nxf6) and now:

2a) 26...Nd3?! 27 Bxd3 cxd3 28 Nxh6 Rae8 29 Nf5 Nf6 (29...Rxe4 30 Rxe4 Rxe4 31 Qh6+ Kg8 32 Bxg7 and White wins) 30 Bxf6 gxf6 31 Nxf6! Rxf6 (31...Rxe1+ 32 Kh2 Qh7 33 Qb2 is winning for White) 32 Rxe8 Qxe8 33 Qg5 Qe6 34 Qh5+ Rh6 (34...Bh6 loses to 35 Nxh6 Rxh6 36 Qg5) 35 Nxh6 Bxh6 36 Rg6 is decisive.

2b) 26...Rae8 27 Qxc4 (27 Nxh6 c3!) 27...a5 28 Qd4 looks good for White. For the moment White is rather tied down by the e-file pin, but he still has an impressive array of pieces pointing at Black’s kingside. In return Black has no material compensation.

3) 25...Nf4? and now:

3a) 26 Nxh6? Rxh6 27 Ng5 is tempting, but fails if Black finds the correct defence. The main lines are:

3a1) 27...Qd7? 28 Qg4! Qxg4 29 Nf7+ Kg8 30 Nxh6+ gxh6 31 Rxg4+ and White wins.

3a2) 27...Rf6? 28 Bxf6 Qxf6 29 Re6 also wins for White.

3a3) 27...Qc7? 28 Re6! (after 28 Ne6 Rxe6 29 Rxe6 Nbd3 30 Rh6+ Kg8 Black defends) 28...Nxe6 (28...Rh4 29 Rg4 Rh5 30 Rxf4 Rxg5 31 Rh6+ Kg8 32 Bh7+ Kh8 33 Bf5+ Kg8 34 Be6+ and White wins) 29 Nxe6 Qf7 30 Nxg7 Bxg7 31 Rxg7 Qxg7 32 Bxg7+ Kxg7 33 Qg4+ Kh8 34 Qg5 is good for White; for example, 34...Re6 35 Qh4+ Kg7 36 Qh7+ Kf6 37 Qg6+ Ke7 38 Qg7+ Kd8 39 Qf8+ Kd7 40 Qf7+ winning the rook.

3a4) 27...Kg8! is the only move, but it is a good one. After 28 Qg4 Nbd5 29 Bxg7 (29 Qf5 Qc7 is also unsatisfactory for White) 29...Bxg7 (29...Bc8? loses to 30 Ne6) 30 Nf7 Qf6 31 Nxh6+ Qxh6 32 Qd7 Rf8 33 Qxb7 Nc3 Black is better.

3b) 26 Nef6! is the spectacular key to success: 26...Rxe1+ (26...Qe7 27 Rxe6 Qxe6 28 Re3 Qf7 29 Nxh6 gxh6 30 Ne8+ Kg8 31 Qg4+ is winning for White) 27 Qxe1 Nbd3 (27...Qe7 loses to 28 Re3 Qf7 29 Nxh6) 28 Bxd3 cxd3 (after 28...Nxd3, 29 Qe6 is decisive) 29 Nxh6 Ne2+ 30 Qxe2 Qxf6 31 Qd2 Qe6 32 Qg5 and the attack is decisive.

4) 25...Nd3! is the correct defence; Black must eliminate one of the dangerous white bishops without delay.

After 26 Bxd3 (26 Nxh6 Rxh6 27 Ng5 Qd7 is unsound for White, while 26 Rxd3 cxd3 27 Qxd3 Nb4 is at best unclear) 26...cxd3 27 Rxd3 Qe8! (27...Qa5 28 Rd4 offers White more chances) the e-file pin gives Black counterplay. A likely continuation is 28 f3 Nb6 29 Rb3 Bxe4 (29...d5 30 Qd4 Nxa4 31 Rxb7 dxe4 32 Rxg7 Bc5 is riskier but is probably also a draw after 33 Rg5+ Bxd4+ 34 Bxd4+ Kh7 35 Rg7+ Kh8 36 Rxe4 Rxe4 37 fxe4 Qd8 38 Be5 Qb6+ 39 Kh2 Rf8, when it is time for perpetual check) 30 Rxb6 Bxf3 31 Rxe6 Bxd1 32 Rxe8 Rxe8 33 Nxh6 with a drawn ending.

I have given this analysis in detail because it might seem a serious error to miss 25...Nd3! – after all, playing the knight to d3 was one of the key elements of Black’s defensive plan. However, when you see the maze of complex variations, the error is not so surprising.

26 Nxh6!

After this spectacular sacrifice Black is in serious trouble. His problem is that he can shut out the dark-squared bishop with ...c3 or the light-squared bishop with ...Nd3, but he cannot do both.

26...c3

There is nothing better:

1) 26...Rxh6 27 Nxd6 wins for White in all variations:

1a) 27...Rxd6 loses to 28 Rxe8 Rxe8 29 Qh5+.

1b) 27...Qxe1+ 28 Qxe1 Rxd6 29 Qe4 wins for White after 29...Nd3 30 Qh4+ Kg8 31 Bxg7 Bxg7 32 Qg4 or 29...Rh6 30 Bc1 Rh5 31 Rg5 Rh6 32 Rf5.

1c) 27...Qd7 28 Qg4 Qxg4 29 Nf7+ Kg8 30 Nxh6+ gxh6 31 Rxg4+ Kf7 32 Bg6+ Kg8 33 Bf5+ Kf7 34 Be6+ and White wins.

1d) 27...Qh5 28 Rg5! Qxd1 29 Nf7+ Kg8 30 Nxh6+ Kh8 31 Rxd1 c3 32 Nf7+ Kg8 33 Bg6 Nf4 (after 33...cxb2, 34 Rh5 mates) 34 Bxc3 Nxg6 35 Bxb4 Kxf7 36 Rd7+ Kf6 37 Rxg6+ Kxg6 38 Rxb7 with two clear extra pawns.

2) After 26...Rc8, we see a key tactical element which arises in several variations. White would like to use the e4-knight in the attack, but moving it drops the e1-rook with check. However, the preliminary 27 Kh2!, threatening 28 Ng5, creates decisive threats. One variation is 27...Rxh6 28 Nxd6 Qd7 29 Qg4! Qxg4 30 Nf7+ Kg8 31 Nxh6+ Kh8 32 Rxg4 c3 33 Nf7+ Kg8 34 Bg6 (with the idea of mating on h8) 34...Rc7 35 Bxc3 Nxc3 36 Rh4 Rxf7 37 Bxf7+ Kxf7 38 Rf4+ Kg6 39 Re6+ Kh7 40 Rxf8 and White wins.

The text-move leaves White’s b1-bishop in a dominant position.

27 Nf5!

Absolutely correct. White can jettison the b2-bishop in the interest of furthering his attack.

27...cxb2

27...Rc8 28 Qg4 cxb2 transposes to line ‘4’ in the note to Black’s 28th move.

28 Qg4!

Once again Kasparov demonstrates the important principle of having all the pieces participating in the attack. White aims to overwhelm Black’s kingside by sheer weight of attacking force.

28...Bc8

There is no defence:

1) 28...Nc3 29 Nf6 Rxe1+ 30 Kh2 mates in a few moves.

2) 28...Qd7 29 Qh5+ Kg8 30 Rg6 Rxe4 (30...Rxg6 loses to 31 Qxg6 Kh8 32 Ng5) 31 Rxe4 Qc7 (31...Rc8 32 Rh4) 32 Rh4 Qc3 33 Nh6+ Kh8 34 Ng4+ Kg8 35 Rf6! mating.

3) 28...g6 29 Kh2 Qd7 (29...Be7 30 Nxe7 Qxe7 31 Nxd6 Rxe1 32 Qxg6 and 29...Kg8 30 Nexd6 are hopeless for Black) 30 Nh4! Bc8 (30...Ne7 loses to 31 Nf6) 31 Nxg6+ Rxg6 32 Qxg6 Qg7 33 Qh5+ Qh6 34 Qf7 and White wins.

4) 28...Rc8 (aiming to block out the light-squared bishop with ...Nc2) 29 Kh2! (again this key move, so that White can move the e4-knight without dropping the rook with check) 29...Nc2 (29...Rc4 30 Nxg7 Rg6 31 Qh5+ Kxg7 32 Nf6! forces a quick mate) 30 Qh4+ Rh6 (30...Kg8 loses to 31 Ng5) 31 Nxh6 gxh6 32 Bxc2 Rxc2 33 Nxd6 with a decisive attack; for example, 33...Qd7 34 Qd4+ Kh7 35 Qd3+ Kh8 36 Qg6 Qg7 37 Nf7+ Kg8 38 Qxc2.

29 Qh4+

29 Nxg7? Rg6! allows Black to fight on.

29...Rh6

Or 29...Kg8 30 Kh2! and there is no defence to the threat of 31 Ng5.

30 Nxh6

30...gxh6

31 Kh2!

Once again this key move, which carries the deadly threats of 32 Nxd6 and 32 Nf6, proves decisive. Even here White could still go wrong; for example, 31 Nf6? Qxe1+ 32 Kh2 Be6 leads to nothing, while 31 Nxd6? Qxe1+ 32 Kh2 Qe6 defends.

31...Qe5

Black cannot avoid a disaster; for example, 31...Ra7 32 Nf6 Qf7 33 Re8 leads to a quick mate.

32 Ng5

Threatening the queen and mate in one.

32...Qf6

33 Re8!

The diagram presents a perfect attacking picture. White’s whole army is swarming around the enemy king.

33...Bf5

34 Qxh6+

The only small slip in an excellent attacking game by Kasparov. 34 Nf7+! Qxf7 (34...Kh7 35 Qxf6 Nxf6 36 Bxf5#) 35 Qxh6+ Bh7 36 Rxa8 would have forced mate in a few moves.

34...Qxh6

35 Nf7+

35...Kh7

36 Bxf5+

36...Qg6

37 Bxg6+

37 Rxg6! would have been even more crushing since 37...Ne7 38 Ng5+ Kh8 39 Rxe7 mates quickly, but of course it makes no difference to the result.

37...Kg7

38 Rxa8

Black could have given up here in view of White’s enormous material advantage.

38...Be7

39 Rb8

39...a5

40 Be4+

40...Kxf7

41 Bxd5+

41...1-0

This game reflects credit on both players. Kasparov’s attack would have blown away a lesser opponent, but Karpov put up an excellent fight. The important point to note is how methodically Kasparov built up his attack, swinging his queen’s rook across to the kingside and repositioning his f3-knight to g4. Up to 25 Ng4, White’s commitment to the attack had been relatively modest (the sacrifice of his d-pawn), but in return he had been able to position every single piece on an attacking square. This was necessary because Black’s kingside was fairly secure, so White needed a considerable superiority of force on the kingside in order to break through. At just this moment Karpov made the decisive error, and Kasparov won brilliantly.

The lessons here are:

1) Bring as many pieces into aggressive positions as you can, before committing yourself irrevocably to the attack.

2) In practice, it is usually more difficult to defend than to attack.

3) A pin against any piece other than the king is not absolute. The ‘pinned’ piece might suddenly move, wreaking havoc.

4) The final breakthrough often requires a sacrifice.

nodes.text:
Bring Up the Reserves

In the case of an attack on the king, the two main factors determining the success of an attack are the balance of forces in the vicinity of the king, and the state of the king’s defences. If the king is severely exposed and lacking defenders, even a relatively modest attacking force (such as a queen and a knight) may be enough to decide the game. However, it is more common for the king to have some defensive pieces and shielding pawns. In this case the attacker needs a considerable local superiority of force in order to break through. It follows that the more pieces take part in the attack, the more likely it is to succeed.

One of the most common attacking errors is to start an attack with insufficient force to drive the attack home. By the time this becomes obvious, it is too late to bring up the reserves. It is therefore a good idea to bring as many pieces as possible into aggressive positions before making an irrevocable commitment to the attack. It may not be possible to do this with every piece, but make whatever preparations you can. It is better to bring a piece into the attack before, rather than after, sacrificing a piece!

The following game is a good example of this principle in action.

G. Kasparov – A. Karpov

World Championship match (game 20), New York/Lyons 1990

Ruy Lopez, Flohr-Zaitsev Variation
For comments on this move, see Game 3.

This symmetrical response is the traditional answer to White’s 1 e4. Although to some extent it has been overshadowed by the immense popularity of the Sicilian Defence (1...c5 – see Game 8, amongst others), it remains a very common and reliable reply. By controlling d4, Black tries to stop White forming a two-abreast pawn-centre.

Overwhelmingly the most popular move, attacking the e5-pawn. If White is obstinate and plays 2 d4 in any case, then after 2...exd4 3 Qxd4 Nc6 White has to lose time with his queen. That is not to say that this line is unplayable – indeed, such strong players as Adams and Morozevich have experimented with it – but the prevailing view is that having to move the queen twice in the first four moves gives Black a relatively easy time. Other second moves, such as 2 f4 (King’s Gambit), 2 Nc3 (Vienna Opening) and 2 Bc4 (Bishop’s Opening) are also relatively rare in contemporary tournament play.

Black to play

Again the most common move, although the counter-attacking 2...Nf6 (Petroff’s Defence) is running it a close second at the higher levels of grandmaster chess. With 2...Nf6, Black is aiming to neutralize White’s advantage by an early liquidation in the centre, and so this move tends to lead to less exciting positions than 2...Nc6, which maintains the tension in the centre for longer. Whether this is an advantage or a defect depends on your point of view. In top-class grandmaster chess, a draw with Black is generally regarded as a desirable objective, hence the popularity of 2...Nf6 at this level. In recent times White has certainly found it hard to prove any real advantage against it.

For players lower down the ranking list, whether to play 2...Nf6 or 2...Nc6 depends much more on personal style. Minute differences in theoretical opinion are much less important than reaching a position of a type that you play well. For top GMs, who can normally play all types of position well, such subjective factors are less important.

This opening, the Ruy Lopez (or Spanish, as it is called in many countries) has been regarded as a sound system for the past 100 years, and this opinion is unlikely to change in the next 100 years. Such classic openings are perennially popular, largely because they are not really a single opening. The Ruy Lopez, for example, consists of a whole complex of different systems; if one falls out of favour, there are plenty of others to provide a viable replacement. The text-move prepares a possible exchange of the c6-knight, thereby removing the only defender of the e5-pawn. White is not immediately threatening to win a pawn (see the note to White’s fourth move), but this possibility is a constant cause of concern for Black.

The main alternative is 3 d4, the Scotch Opening (see Game 16), which is similar to 2 d4 except that White avoids the problems associated with an early queen development (after 3...exd4 4 Nxd4 Nxd4 5 Qxd4 it is not easy for Black to harass the queen). While Kasparov has had some success with the Scotch, other players do not seem able to emulate his results.

Black to play

This is the most popular reply. At this moment White cannot win a pawn by taking on c6, so Black takes the opportunity to kick the bishop back to a4. Then Black will have the option of removing the attack against c6 by playing ...b5. Another sound system is 3...Nf6 (the Berlin Defence) although, like 2...Nf6, this line often leads to rather dull positions.

4 Bxc6 dxc6 5 0-0 (after 5 Nxe5, Black regains the pawn by 5...Qd4) genuinely threatens the e5-pawn, but Black has various possible counters: 5...f6, 5...Bg4, 5...Qd6 and 5...Bd6 are all reasonable replies. This Exchange Variation suits white-players who like quiet positions and endgame play.

Black develops his knight to the most natural square, at the same time attacking White’s e4-pawn.

5 0-0

White is able to ignore the attack and continue his development, because if Black takes on e4, White can regain his pawn by force.

Black to play

This move leads to the Closed Variation of the Ruy Lopez. The main alternative is 5...Nxe4, the Open Variation. Although the Open Variation is not especially popular, there is nothing really wrong with it; after 6 d4 b5 (6...exd4 is dubious since after 7 Re1 d5 8 Nxd4 both black knights are awkwardly pinned) 7 Bb3 the greedy 7...exd4? 8 Re1 d5 favours White since 9 Nc3! Be6 (9...dxc3?! 10 Bxd5 is even worse for Black, while 9...Be7? loses a piece to 10 Bxd5) 10 Nxe4 dxe4 11 Rxe4 Be7 12 Bxe6 fxe6 13 Nxd4 gives Black nothing to show for his extremely weak e-pawn. Therefore Black does best to give the pawn back by 7...d5 8 dxe5 Be6, when an interesting position arises in which Black’s main asset is his active knight on e4. Despite many decades of analysis, the best continuation for White is far from clear, although most grandmasters seem to think that White can retain an edge.

White defends the e4-pawn and does not allow Black a second chance to take on e4. This move is stronger than the obvious 6 Nc3, because White still aims to set up a two-abreast pawn-centre with c3 and d4, and so he should avoid blocking the c-pawn. This plan is time-consuming, but if White succeeds in establishing and maintaining his pawn-centre, then he will have promising long-term chances.
White was really threatening to win a pawn by 7 Bxc6 dxc6 8 Nxe5, so this is the appropriate moment to drive the bishop away.
Black to play

Round about here Black must decide how he is going to counter White’s plan. The text-move indicates that Black intends to respond by reinforcing his grip on e5, to make sure that it can resist the pressure White will exert on that square when he plays d4.

The main alternative is 7...0-0 8 c3 d5, the famous Marshall Attack, whereby Black offers a pawn in return for a lead in development and kingside pressure. These days the Marshall Attack is still played occasionally, but is not regarded as especially fearsome. Not only is it possible (albeit double-edged) to accept the pawn, but also White can avoid the Marshall entirely by playing 8 a4 instead of 8 c3.
This not only prepares d4, but also gives the bishop a chance to slip back to c2 in case it is attacked by ...Na5.
Black proceeds with his development and brings his king into safety.

White continues his preparation for d4. If he plays 9 d4 at once, Black replies 9...Bg4 and White must make some concession. Black’s threat is to double White’s pawns by ...Bxf3 (since the reply Qxf3 would cost White his d-pawn). There are then two main possibilities for White. The first is 10 d5, but this gives up one of the main benefits of a two-abreast pawn-centre – its flexibility. The second is 10 Be3 but the bishop is not ideally placed here since it blocks the e-file. After 10...exd4 11 cxd4 Na5 12 Bc2 c5 Black’s lead in development compensates for White’s slightly superior central control.

Black to play

This is a key decision-point for Black. He cannot hold up White’s d4 advance any longer, so he has to start thinking about the post-d4 world. Although Black has to face the fact that White will be better placed in the centre, Black has a lead in development as compensation. He must put this to good use, because given time White will catch up in development, when Black will simply be worse because of the situation in the centre, which inherently favours White. There are two main plans Black can adopt:

1) To attack White’s centre using his pawns. Since ...d5 is unlikely to be playable owing to the weakness of e5, this means using ...c5 to put pressure on White’s d4-pawn, hoping to force a clarification of the central pawn-structure. There are two ways to implement this plan:

1a) 9...Na5 10 Bc2 c5 11 d4 Qc7 is the first. Black has already played ...c5, and he will quickly develop his remaining pieces by ...Bd7 and ...Rac8 (possibly with ...cxd4 thrown in). The pressure against d4 and along the c-file is quite awkward for White. As usual in the Closed Ruy Lopez, the question is whether White can withstand the temporary pressure and complete his development with his centre intact, or whether he has to play d5 or dxe5 under unfavourable circumstances.

1b) The other way is 9...Nb8 10 d4 Nbd7, the Breyer Defence. This line may look odd but it is actually quite logical. Black manoeuvres his knight to defend e5 from a square that does not block the thematic ...c5. After 11 Nbd2 Bb7 another benefit of Black’s knight manoeuvre is revealed: on d7, the knight does not block the long diagonal and therefore White’s e-pawn comes under unwelcome pressure. In order for White to complete his queenside development, he must move his d2-knight to f1, but at the moment this is impossible. Therefore White usually plays 12 Bc2, followed by Nf1-g3, but of course this represents a loss of time and gives Black the opportunity to strike out with a timely ...c5.

2) Black’s second plan is to interfere with White’s development using piece pressure against White’s centre. This means mounting a rapid attack against e4, so as to prevent the important Nbd2-f1 manoeuvre (rather as in the Breyer Defence above). Thus the bishop should move to b7; the attack against e4 is currently masked by the c6-knight, but White must still bear it in mind.
White has succeeded in building a good pawn-centre, but the cost is a lag in development.

White to play

Black intends ...Bf8 to step up the pressure against e4. At first sight the text-move looks wrong, because White can play 11 Ng5, forcing Black to return his rook to f8. However, White cannot make any use of the knight on g5, and has nothing better than to return it to f3. This means that a draw by repetition is in White’s hands, if he wants it. Many games have finished like this, but most players are unwilling to concede the advantage of the white pieces so easily!

The pawn on c3 means that d2 is the best square available for the knight, despite the obvious defect of blocking in the c1-bishop and thus the a1-rook. If White could complete the manoeuvre Nf1-g3, then he would be able to support the e4-pawn without blocking in the bishop, so Black should try to prevent this.
Black is just in time to set up an attack on e4.

12 Nf1 is bad because of 12...exd4 13 cxd4 Na5, when White has no good reply to the triple attack on e4 (not, however, 13...Nxe4? due to 14 Rxe4! Rxe4 15 Ng5 Re7 16 Qh5 with a very strong attack). 12 Ng5 is again ineffective, this time because of 12...Re7. 12 Bc2 appears logical, the aim being to free the d2-knight to move to f1 and g3. However, 13 Nf1 isn’t really a threat, because after 13...exd4 14 cxd4 Nb4 Black threatens both the e-pawn and 15...Nxc2, eliminating a useful bishop.

Therefore, White switches to a different plan; he intends to develop queenside play while leaving the knight on d2. The text-move is useful because White’s rook may have to stay at a1 for a considerable time while he is sorting out the development of the c1-bishop. Playing a4 introduces the possibility that the rook may enter play along the a-file. Moreover, White may be able to step up the pressure against the b5-pawn and force Black to clarify the queenside pawn-structure.

Black to play
Observing that White has few useful moves, Black decides to spend a tempo preventing Ng5. However, this move is by no means forced and both 12...g6 and 12...Qd7 are playable.
White has indeed run out of useful moves and must now concede this voluntary retreat. The intention is 14 Bd3, forcing Black to make an awkward decision regarding the attack on the b5-pawn.

This move is clearly very double-edged, because Black surrenders his central strongpoint on e5. On the other hand, it opens various possibilities for active piece-play. The e8-rook is now directly attacking the e4-pawn, and Black will be able to step up his pressure on White’s centre with gain of tempo. 13...Rb8 and 13...Nb8 are playable alternatives, although both have a slightly passive appearance.

Black to play

A hidden benefit of this capture is that it opens the third rank, increasing White’s chances of activating the a1-rook via a3.

Black clears the way for ...c5, to try to break up White’s centre. Unlike the lines 9...Na5 and 9...Nb8 discussed in the note to Black’s 9th move, in this case Black frees the c-pawn with gain of tempo and occupies an excellent outpost with his knight.
White must keep his bishop, since it is currently one of his few active pieces.

It is perfectly natural for Black to strike at White’s centre like this, but the move has both good and bad features. On the minus side, after White’s reply the b7-bishop will be shut out of play. On the plus side, Black gains space and stabilizes the central pawn-structure.

Black to play

The only challenging move, maintaining a 2-1 central pawn-majority. The central pawn-structure now resembles one arising from a Benoni (1 d4 Nf6 2 c4 c5 3 d5 e6 4 Nc3 exd5 5 cxd5 d6) rather than a Ruy Lopez. However, the arrangement of pieces is not typical for a Benoni so we cannot transplant typical ideas and plans from the Benoni into the current situation.

In the diagram position, White’s ambitions lie mainly on the kingside. Although currently White’s pieces are not menacing Black’s kingside, it only requires a few moves – Ra3, Nh2 and Rg3 – and the threats already look quite serious. Black, for his part, will aim to make use of his queenside majority, for example by ...Nd7, ...c4 and ...Nc5 (aiming for d3), possibly coupled with ...g6 and ...Bg7. The arrival of a knight on d3 is a key feature of this system, not only because it is an excellent outpost in itself, but also because it would block the activity of the b1-bishop and so reduce the force of White’s attack.
A flexible move. The first idea is ...c4 and ...Nc5, as mentioned previously; the second idea is revealed with Black’s next move.

Black to play

White makes the first ominous move towards Black’s kingside.

Black ups the stakes with this double-edged move. One of the main principles of defending against an attack on the king is not to create unnecessary weaknesses, yet here is a top-class player lunging forwards in contravention of precisely this rule. In view of the battering Black receives in this game, it might seem that justice was indeed dispensed, yet in fact this move is not at all bad. Certainly, it is very risky, yet it has many positive features. It is quite likely that the e4-pawn will disappear (either through exf5 or ...fxe4), but then the d5-pawn will be weak. If Black can take it, then his formerly inactive b7-bishop will suddenly become very powerful. If White plays exf5, then an exchange of rooks will tend to reduce the force of White’s attack.

It is true that if White’s attack were further advanced, Black could not get away with a move such as ...f5, but White’s threats are as yet quite far away and so Black still has time to break up White’s centre. Indeed, ...f5 can be seen as a pre-emptive strike to fight for activity before White’s attack becomes too strong.

The alternative is 17...c4, aiming to play ...Nc5. This is perfectly playable and possibly safer than the text-move, although it does have the defect of allowing the f3-knight to occupy d4.

Even today, nobody knows whether this is the strongest move. In addition to the text-move, 18 g4, 18 exf5, 18 e5 and (most recently) 18 Nh2 have all been tried with varying degrees of success. Perhaps this last move is most logical as White does not yet commit his rook to a particular square; it might move to e3, f3 or g3 according to circumstances. In any case, White’s main aim is to bring his remaining pieces into the attack without allowing too many exchanges. When attacking, it is essential to have enough firepower available to finish the job. What constitutes ‘enough firepower’ varies a lot from position to position. If the enemy kingside is weakened and lacks defensive pieces, then even a small attacking force may be enough. However, in practice it often happens that the attack is being conducted against a relatively solid kingside, and in this case more pieces must be brought to bear to ensure success.

White’s main problem is to coordinate his forces for the attack. The c1-bishop in particular is quite inactive, and is likely to remain so because the knight must stay on d2 to be ready to recapture on e4. Kasparov therefore moves his a3-rook to a more active position, preparing to activate the bishop by b3 and Bb2.

Black to play

The situation in the centre remains fluid for a number of moves, the reason being that neither player wishes to resolve the e4-f5 tension. Black, it is true, can win the d5-pawn by playing ...fxe4, but this capture brings the d2-knight to an aggressive position with gain of tempo. It also gives both white bishops an obstructed view of Black’s kingside. In the end, Black may be forced to make this capture, but he intends to make as many constructive moves as he can first. Likewise, the move exf5 would be a concession by White. True, this avoids losing a pawn, but more importantly it loses time, since the knight stays on d2, and the pawn on f5 can actually obstruct White’s attack. Thus White also would prefer to find constructive moves that do not involve resolving the central tension.

The alternatives to the text-move are:

1) 18...f4!? attempts to block the position. After 19 R3e2 Ne5 20 Nf1 Nxf3+ 21 gxf3 Qh4 a very unclear position arises. If Black can cement his hold on e5 then he will stand well, but there is a danger that White will unleash all the energy in his position by playing e5 himself.

2) 18...fxe4?! is a concession since after 19 Rxe4 (19 Bxe4 is doubtful because of 19...Nf6, while 19 Nxe4 Nxd5 20 R3e2 N7f6 is unclear) 19...Rxe4 20 Nxe4 Nxd5 21 Qe2 White’s c1-bishop has entered the attack. The fact that Black has won a pawn is less important. White now threatens 22 Nxd6, and Black has defensive difficulties; for example, after 21...N7f6 22 Nxf6+ Qxf6 (22...Nxf6 23 Qe6+ Kh8 24 Nh4 is crushing) 23 Qe4 Black can hardly prevent White’s queen from landing on h7 (23...g6 is met by 24 Nh4!).

White decides to clear the third rank after all, and introduces the possibility of a later Ng4 (after the f5-pawn has disappeared) to eliminate a kingside defender. If this is really White’s best move, then it was surely better to play Nh2 last move, as the rook might want to move from a3 to g3 in one go.

19 exf5?! is wrong; after 19...Rxe3 20 Rxe3 Nbxd5 Black gains time and the f5-pawn blocks White’s attack.

Black to play

Only here does Black commit a perceptible inaccuracy. Tidying-up moves such as Kg1-h1 and Kc1-b1 (or ...Kg8-h8 and ...Kc8-b8 by Black) are of course common enough, but one must be sure that the gain is worth the tempo spent on the move. This is more likely to be the case when the position is relatively quiet and the tempo is not especially significant. Here, with a violent storm about to break over Black’s kingside, it looks very risky to spend a tempo on this move, which actually has little clear benefit. If White is going to hack his way into the kingside sacrificially, it probably isn’t going to make much difference whether the king is on g8 or h8. After the text-move, Black must proceed with extreme accuracy in order not to be wiped out by White’s attack.

19...fxe4 is again wrong. After 20 Nxe4 Nbxd5 (20...Nfxd5 21 Rg3 is extremely dangerous for Black; for example, 21...Kh8 22 Bxh6 gxh6 23 Qg4 Ne7 24 Nf6 with a quick mate) 21 Nxf6+ Nxf6 22 Rxe8 Nxe8 23 Qd3 Nf6 24 Ng4 White has an extremely strong attack.

Black should have played 19...Qd7 connecting the rooks, supporting the b5-pawn and offering some lateral defence along the second rank. I do not want to go into a deep discussion of what is still opening theory; suffice it to say that White has not been able to prove any advantage after 19...Qd7. This move was played in a later Kasparov-Karpov game (Euwe Memorial, Amsterdam 1991) and on that occasion Black equalized and the game ended in a draw.

41...1-0

This game reflects credit on both players. Kasparov’s attack would have blown away a lesser opponent, but Karpov put up an excellent fight. The important point to note is how methodically Kasparov built up his attack, swinging his queen’s rook across to the kingside and repositioning his f3-knight to g4. Up to 25 Ng4, White’s commitment to the attack had been relatively modest (the sacrifice of his d-pawn), but in return he had been able to position every single piece on an attacking square. This was necessary because Black’s kingside was fairly secure, so White needed a considerable superiority of force on the kingside in order to break through. At just this moment Karpov made the decisive error, and Kasparov won brilliantly.

The lessons here are:

1) Bring as many pieces into aggressive positions as you can, before committing yourself irrevocably to the attack.

2) In practice, it is usually more difficult to defend than to attack.

3) A pin against any piece other than the king is not absolute. The ‘pinned’ piece might suddenly move, wreaking havoc.

4) The final breakthrough often requires a sacrifice.
